content.text: A sandbox environment is an isolated, ephemeral execution context spun up specifically to run a single round of tests — typically one per pull request — and torn down once those tests complete. Unlike a shared staging environment that persists between deploys, a sandbox exists for a bounded time window, carries no history from previous runs, and is fully separated from every other concurrent sandbox at the network level.

For agentic AI systems, isolation is not optional. An agent that runs against external APIs, writes files, sends messages, or modifies database rows leaves real side effects. Without isolation, two concurrent test runs can corrupt each other's state, a misconfigured test can hit a production endpoint, or a tool call can consume real API quota. Sandboxes eliminate those risks by giving every test run its own contained world in which it can do anything — including failing loudly — without touching anything real.
The most common implementations are Kubernetes namespace-per-PR (each pull request gets its own namespace with its own copies of all services), Docker Compose-based local sandboxes (a single
brings up the full agent stack plus all its dependencies), and ephemeral cloud environments provisioned by CI (GitHub Actions creating an isolated VPC or EKS cluster per run).

Agents are qualitatively different from stateless web services. A traditional web service test that fails might leave a database row behind. An agent test that fails might have sent an email, written to a file system, triggered a downstream webhook, or consumed 50,000 tokens of LLM API quota. The blast radius of a test failure is much larger, and the contamination is harder to detect and clean up.

Shared test environments compound this problem. When multiple engineers push code simultaneously, their agent tests run against the same mock services, the same test databases, and the same internal endpoints. Race conditions, residual state from a previous run, and quota exhaustion from parallel workloads all become daily friction. Sandbox isolation moves each test run into its own universe, so interactions between test runs become structurally impossible rather than something each engineer must reason about.
Two PRs can run their agent tests simultaneously without any possibility of their side effects interfering with each other.
Real external APIs blocked by default.
Network egress is restricted inside the sandbox, so an agent that accidentally calls a real third-party API hits a connection refusal rather than silently consuming real resources.
Because the sandbox is created fresh, there is no residual state from a previous test run. Flaky tests caused by stale data are eliminated.
A namespace-per-PR sandbox can mirror the exact service graph of production (agent service, tool servers, message queue, database) without risking any production data.
Engineers can push freely without coordinating on who owns the shared staging environment, dramatically reducing wait time in busy teams.
Sandbox teardown stops resource billing the moment CI completes. Unlike a persistent staging environment that runs 24/7, ephemeral sandboxes only cost money during the test window.
Creating a Kubernetes namespace, pulling container images, and initialising databases takes time — often 2–5 minutes before the first test runs. This slows the inner feedback loop.
Namespace-per-PR requires a working Kubernetes cluster, a namespace controller, and tooling to inject per-namespace hostnames and credentials into the agent under test.
Mock services that simulate external APIs (Stripe, Slack, third-party LLM endpoints) must be kept up to date as those APIs evolve, adding ongoing maintenance work.

Ephemeral sandboxes run against mock services and synthetic data. A shared staging environment that receives real traffic samples is still needed to catch integration issues that mocks cannot reproduce.
On a busy team with many concurrent PRs, running a full Kubernetes namespace per PR can be expensive. Resource quotas, shared node pools, and auto-scaling are needed to keep costs predictable.

When a developer opens or updates a pull request, the CI system (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or similar) triggers a pipeline. The first job in that pipeline calls a sandbox controller — a small operator or Helm chart — that creates a new Kubernetes namespace named after the PR (for example

). Into that namespace the controller deploys: the agent service image built from the PR branch, all internal tool servers (database proxy, file store, search service), and a set of mock servers that stand in for any external API the agent uses.

A network policy restricts the namespace so that pods inside it can communicate with each other freely but cannot reach any address outside the cluster. This means an agent that tries to call a real external API (because a developer forgot to point it at the mock) receives a connection refused error rather than silently hitting the real service.

The test runner executes agent test scenarios against the in-namespace agent endpoint. Once the run completes — pass or fail — a cleanup job deletes the namespace and all its resources. If the run takes too long (a CI timeout), the cleanup job runs anyway via a finalizer, preventing resource leaks.

attrs.alt: Sandbox architecture: CI trigger creates a Sandbox Orchestrator which spins up three isolated namespaces (PR-42, PR-43, PR-44) each containing an Agent Container and Mock Services, all results flow to CI Pass/Fail and namespaces are deleted on completion.

content.text: Each pull request gets an independent Kubernetes namespace. The namespace contains the agent image from the PR branch alongside lightweight mock services that simulate external APIs. Namespaces share the same node pool but are network-isolated from each other: a pod in
your agent makes tool calls with real side effects (file writes, database mutations, API calls) that would contaminate a shared environment.
multiple engineers regularly push concurrent PRs and shared staging has become a bottleneck or source of flaky tests.
Use Docker Compose sandboxes for local development
when engineers need to run the full agent stack on their laptop without a Kubernetes cluster.
for unit tests that mock all dependencies at the code level — those are already isolated and run in milliseconds with no infrastructure overhead.
Do not replace staging with sandboxes.
A sandbox runs against mocks. A shared staging environment is needed for testing with real external integrations under controlled conditions before production deploy.
Write mock services for every external API the agent calls. A minimal mock is an HTTP server that returns pre-configured responses. Tools like WireMock or a simple Flask/FastAPI stub work well.
Package the agent and all mock services as Docker images. The agent image is built from the PR branch; mock images are pinned releases.
Write a Helm chart or Kustomize overlay that defines the full sandbox: namespace, deployments, services, network policy, and config maps. Make the PR number a template variable.
In your CI pipeline, add a
Run the agent integration tests against the in-namespace agent endpoint. Use the namespace-scoped service DNS (
) so test URLs resolve to the correct sandbox.
job that runs regardless of test outcome (using
in GitHub Actions) and deletes the namespace:
Set resource quotas on each namespace to cap CPU and memory usage, preventing a runaway test from starving other sandboxes on the same cluster.

# .github/workflows/sandbox.yml
name: Agent Sandbox CI

on:
  pull_request:

env:
  PR_NS: pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}

jobs:
  sandbox-up:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Build agent image
        run: docker build -t agent:${{ github.sha }} .
      - name: Push to registry
        run: docker push registry.example.com/agent:${{ github.sha }}
      - name: Deploy sandbox namespace
        run: |
          helm upgrade --install sandbox-${{ env.PR_NS }} ./charts/sandbox \
            --namespace ${{ env.PR_NS }} \
            --create-namespace \
            --set agent.image.tag=${{ github.sha }} \
            --set networkPolicy.denyEgress=true \
            --wait --timeout 5m

  integration-tests:
    needs: sandbox-up
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run agent integration tests
        env:
          AGENT_URL: http://agent.${{ env.PR_NS }}.svc.cluster.local:8080
        run: pytest tests/integration/ -v --timeout=120

  sandbox-down:
    needs: [sandbox-up, integration-tests]
    if: always()
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Delete sandbox namespace
        run: kubectl delete namespace ${{ env.PR_NS }} --ignore-not-found

# docker-compose.sandbox.yml — full local agent sandbox
version: "3.9"

services:
  agent:
    build: .
    environment:
      - ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY}
      - SEARCH_API_URL=http://mock-search:8001
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@db:5432/agentdb
    depends_on:
      - mock-search
      - mock-llm
      - db
    networks:
      - sandbox

  mock-search:
    image: wiremock/wiremock:3.3.1
    volumes:
      - ./tests/mocks/search:/home/wiremock/mappings
    networks:
      - sandbox

  mock-llm:
    image: wiremock/wiremock:3.3.1
    volumes:
      - ./tests/mocks/llm:/home/wiremock/mappings
    networks:
      - sandbox

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: agentdb
      POSTGRES_USER: user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: pass
    networks:
      - sandbox

networks:
  sandbox:
    # internal: true disables external connectivity
    internal: true

# charts/sandbox/templates/networkpolicy.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-external-egress
  namespace: {{ .Release.Namespace }}
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    # Allow DNS resolution
    - ports:
        - port: 53
          protocol: UDP
    # Allow intra-namespace communication only
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: {{ .Release.Namespace }}

An agentic code-review service that uses an LLM to analyse pull requests and then posts comments via the GitHub API underwent a difficult period where tests were regularly corrupting each other. Two engineers pushing PRs simultaneously caused the agent under PR A's test to post comments to the PR being tested by PR B's run, because both used the same shared GitHub test repository.

The team switched to a namespace-per-PR setup. Each sandbox included the agent service, a WireMock server that stubbed the GitHub API, and a local Postgres instance. The network policy blocked any real calls to api.github.com. Test execution time increased by three minutes (sandbox spin-up), but cross-contamination incidents dropped to zero. Engineers stopped having to coordinate test runs, and the CI pass rate on main improved markedly because residual state from previous runs was eliminated.
Think of a sandbox environment as a

in a hospital. Each new patient (PR) is placed in their own room with everything they need. They can do anything inside the room — it does not affect other patients. When treatment ends, the room is cleaned and reset for the next patient. The hospital's main ward (production) remains completely untouched. The quarantine design is not a precaution against a specific known infection; it is a structural guarantee that contagion cannot happen regardless of what the patient does.

Creating a Kubernetes namespace but omitting the NetworkPolicy is a frequent oversight. Without the egress deny rule, a misconfigured agent can still reach real external endpoints. Always apply a default-deny egress policy and explicitly allowlist only intra-namespace and DNS traffic.
If the sandbox teardown step is gated on test success, a failing test leaves the namespace running and accumulating cloud cost. Always configure cleanup as
in CI so it runs regardless of outcome. Add a periodic garbage-collection job that deletes namespaces older than two hours as a backstop.

Mock services pinned to an outdated API response shape will silently pass tests while the real integration breaks. Version the mock mapping files alongside the agent code. When the real external API changes, update both the agent integration code and the mock responses in the same PR.
Ignoring Spin-Up Cost in CI Time Budgets

A sandbox that takes five minutes to provision before any test runs is unacceptable in a tight CI feedback loop. Mitigate with pre-pulled images on cluster nodes (DaemonSet pre-puller), pre-warmed database snapshots, and Helm chart optimisations that parallelise pod start. Target sub-90-second sandbox ready state.
in the namespace NetworkPolicy from day one. Explicitly add allowlist rules only for services the tests genuinely need to reach.
) and add a periodic cleanup CronJob to catch any namespaces that slip through due to CI runner crashes.
(CPU, memory, number of pods) to prevent a runaway agent from starving other sandbox namespaces on the shared cluster.
rather than migration scripts to keep sandbox DB initialisation fast. Store the snapshot as an OCI layer cached in the registry.
Version mock service mappings with the agent code.
When the external API schema changes, update the mock mapping and the agent code in the same PR.
) that returns the PR number and git SHA, making it easy to correlate CI logs with a specific sandbox instance.

At low PR volumes (under 10 concurrent), a single shared Kubernetes cluster with resource quotas handles namespace-per-PR comfortably. At scale (50+ concurrent PRs), the cluster node pool needs to auto-scale, and image pull times become a significant contributor to sandbox start latency.
Run a DaemonSet that pulls the latest base images onto all nodes nightly, so that only the thin application layer needs pulling at sandbox-up time.
Mock service images are the same across all sandboxes. Cache them aggressively in the registry and at the node level.
Cap each namespace at a realistic maximum (e.g. 4 CPU, 8 Gi RAM) so that a single sandbox does not prevent cluster auto-scaler from reaching its limit.
If each agent test in the sandbox makes real LLM calls (even against an in-cluster mock), run them with concurrency caps to avoid overwhelming the mock server.
Package the sandbox as a Helm chart or Kustomize overlay with the PR number as a variable. Helm's upgrade/install atomicity makes it easy to retry a partial sandbox deploy.
An HTTP mock server that loads response mappings from JSON files. Ideal for mocking external REST APIs (GitHub, Stripe, Slack) inside the sandbox.

A lightweight virtual Kubernetes cluster that runs inside a namespace. Provides stronger isolation than a plain namespace and lets each sandbox have its own control plane objects (CRDs, etc.) without requiring a separate cluster.
Developer tools that let a process running locally intercept traffic intended for a pod in the sandbox cluster, enabling rapid local iteration without a full redeploy.
A built-in concept for scoping environment-specific secrets and protection rules to a deployment target. Pair with namespace-per-PR to inject the right credentials into the sandbox.

Sandbox environments must not become a path for secrets to leak. Each sandbox needs only the credentials required for the test run itself — not staging or production credentials. Use a separate set of sandbox-only API keys (which can be revoked independently) and inject them from a CI secret store rather than from the same vault path used by production.
Separate sandbox secrets from production secrets.
A bug in the agent under test should not be able to exfiltrate production credentials even if it escapes the mock service layer.
As the agent acquires new tool integrations, verify that the allowlist does not accidentally permit egress to real production endpoints.
Run container image vulnerability scans on the PR branch image before deploying it to the sandbox. A vulnerable image should block the sandbox from starting.
pod security standard to sandbox namespaces to prevent privilege escalation from a container breakout.
Agents have real side effects — file writes, API calls, database mutations — which make isolation a necessity rather than a convenience for integration testing.
Kubernetes namespace-per-PR is the standard pattern at team scale; Docker Compose sandboxes serve the same purpose for local development without a cluster.
Network egress must be denied by default in every sandbox, with only intra-namespace and DNS traffic allowed, to prevent agents from accidentally reaching real external services.
Sandbox teardown must be unconditional (run on both pass and fail) and backed by a periodic garbage-collection job to prevent resource leaks.
Resource quotas per namespace protect the shared cluster from a single runaway sandbox consuming all available compute.
Mock service response mappings must be versioned alongside agent code; stale mocks are a silent reliability hazard that surfaces only when the real external API is exercised.
Sandboxes complement, not replace, a shared staging environment — staging provides real integration testing; sandboxes provide safe, parallel, per-PR isolation.
